definition: Describing phenomena as not fit to be taken as self, owned as self, or identified as I or mine.
notes: The repository keeps not-self as the default because it is the most stable English for canonical formulae and avoids turning the teaching into either a metaphysical slogan or a vague ethical compliment. The project treats anattā primarily as a practical and analytical designation: what is impermanent and dependently arisen is not fit to be appropriated as self. Buddhadāsa-sensitive handling requires explicit resistance to reifying a hidden self or to overshooting into nihilistic no-self rhetoric. The phrase free of I and mine is retained as explanatory prose, not as the default doctrinal label. Translators should keep the canonical not-self label stable in three-marks and aggregate passages and should use explanatory paraphrases only when the prose explicitly needs them.

context_rules.context: In direct doctrinal statements and three-characteristics formulations
In explanatory prose unpacking the predicate force of the term
context_rules.notes: Useful when English needs a more explicit sentence shape.

context_rules.context: In practical OSF or Buddhadāsa-sensitive explanation of release from appropriation
context_rules.notes: Keep the canonical label while the notes explain its relation to I and mine.

context_rules.context: When the point is freedom from identification and ownership rather than a doctrinal formula
context_rules.notes: Allowed explanatory alternate, not the default translation.

example_phrases.pali: netaṃ mama, nesohamasmi, na me so attā
example_phrases.translation: this is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self
example_phrases.notes: Practical formula that explains the force of not-self.

example_phrases.pali: yaṃ aniccaṃ taṃ dukkhaṃ; yaṃ dukkhaṃ tad anattā
example_phrases.translation: what is impermanent is dissatisfaction; what is dissatisfaction is not-self
example_phrases.notes: Shows the close doctrinal link with impermanence and dissatisfaction.

version_notes: Revised to block both metaphysical self-language and nihilistic no-self drift, with stronger guidance for practical not-self interpretation.
